content.text: Running autonomous AI agents on ephemeral, pay-per-invocation compute is one of the most operationally efficient ways to deploy agentic systems at scale. Because agent workloads are inherently bursty — idle between user turns, then briefly intensive during a reasoning step or tool call — the serverless execution model maps naturally to their demand profile.

is an AI agent whose orchestration logic, tool-calling, and response generation are hosted on a Function-as-a-Service (FaaS) platform or a container platform that scales to zero — such as AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, Azure Functions, or container runtimes like AWS Fargate and Google Cloud Run. Instead of a permanently running process, each agent
(receive input, call LLM, decide action, execute tool) maps to one or more short-lived function invocations.

The defining property is that the platform manages capacity automatically: no functions run (and no cost is incurred) when the agent has no work to do, and thousands of parallel instances spin up within seconds when demand spikes. This is fundamentally different from a containerized agent service where a pod sits idle between conversations consuming memory and CPU.
The core engineering challenge is that agents maintain

across multiple reasoning steps and user turns, while FaaS runtimes are stateless. Serverless agent architectures solve this by externalizing all state to a durable store — DynamoDB, Redis, or S3 — and loading it at the start of each function invocation. The function runs its step, writes an updated checkpoint, and exits. The next invocation (which may run on a completely different physical machine) loads the checkpoint and continues seamlessly.

Most production agent deployments face extremely uneven traffic. A customer-support agent might handle zero conversations at 3 AM and a hundred simultaneously at 10 AM. A research agent triggered by nightly batch jobs runs for minutes and then sits idle for 23 hours. Traditional 24/7 servers waste resources on idle time. Serverless eliminates that waste: compute cost is proportional to actual agent activity, not reserved capacity.

Beyond cost, serverless simplifies the operations story considerably. There are no servers to patch, no capacity-planning spreadsheets for expected peak load, and no deployment pipelines that require a rolling restart of long-running containers. A code change to the agent orchestrator deploys in seconds via a zip upload or container push, and the platform automatically routes new invocations to the updated code. For teams shipping agent iterations rapidly — which is typical in the early phases of an agentic product — this dramatically shortens the feedback loop.
FaaS billing is per-request and per-GB-second of execution. An agent that runs 5 minutes per hour costs nothing for the other 55 minutes.
the platform spins up concurrent instances to match demand, handling traffic spikes without any manual intervention or pre-provisioning.
SQS-triggered functions automatically retry on failure and route poison messages to a dead-letter queue for inspection, reducing the amount of reliability code that needs to be written.
native triggers from dozens of AWS/GCP/Azure services mean agents can react to real-world events without additional glue code.
deploying a new agent version takes seconds and does not require a running process to be restarted or a load balancer to be drained.
no OS patching, no cluster management, no capacity planning. The platform handles infrastructure reliability, multi-AZ redundancy, and hardware replacement.
each function invocation runs in a clean environment, preventing inter-request state contamination — a common bug in long-running agent servers.

a function that has not run recently requires a new execution environment to be provisioned. For Python Lambda functions, cold starts typically add 200–800 ms. Large dependency packages (e.g., including PyTorch or large LangChain chains) can push cold start into the 2–5 second range.
every piece of agent state — message history, tool results, memory — must be written to an external store and read back on every invocation. This adds latency and requires careful schema design.

AWS Lambda caps at 15 minutes per invocation; Google Cloud Functions at 60 minutes; Azure Functions at 10 minutes (Consumption plan). Agents with very long reasoning chains must checkpoint and re-invoke before the deadline.
distributed, ephemeral execution makes tracing a multi-step agent run across invocations significantly harder than stepping through a single long-running process in a debugger.
a complex LLM call with tool use can take 30–120 seconds. Multiple sequential LLM calls in a single invocation can exhaust the timeout quickly, especially for models with long reasoning chains.
high-throughput deployments hit both the platform's reserved concurrency limit and the upstream LLM provider's tokens-per-minute rate limit simultaneously, requiring careful throttling.

response streaming from an LLM to a user requires a long-lived connection. Lambda's maximum synchronous response payload is 6 MB and streaming support via Lambda Function URLs adds architectural complexity.

A serverless agent run starts when an event triggers the orchestrator function. The function loads the agent's current state — message history, tool results accumulated so far, and metadata like the current step index — from the external state store using a
key embedded in the event payload.
Each invocation of the orchestrator function executes one full
step. The function calls the LLM with the current message history and the available tools schema. If the model returns a

block, the function either (a) executes the tool call directly if it is fast (under a few seconds) or (b) dispatches the tool call to a dedicated tool-runner function asynchronously and returns, writing a checkpoint that records the pending tool invocation. When the tool runner completes, it places the result back on a queue or writes it to the state store and triggers the orchestrator again.

If the model returns a final text response with no tool calls, the orchestrator writes the final answer back to the state store, publishes it to the response channel (an API Gateway connection, an SQS queue, or a callback URL), and exits. The total round-trip from event receipt to final response consists of N function invocations, where N is the number of reasoning steps the agent takes.
The agent's progress is modeled as a finite state machine persisted in the external store. Typical states include:
— event received, orchestrator not yet invoked or waiting to re-invoke
— waiting for one or more tool runners to complete
Storing the state machine status alongside the message history enables dead-letter queue handlers to resume stuck sessions and monitoring dashboards to show live agent progress without tailing logs.

For Lambda, cost is calculated across two dimensions: request count and GB-seconds of memory consumed. For a typical orchestrator function allocated 1 GB of memory running for an average of 8 seconds per step:

attrs.latex: \text{cost per step} = \underbrace{N_{\text{req}} \times \$0.0000002}_{\text{request charge}} + \underbrace{8 \text{ s} \times 1 \text{ GB} \times \$0.0000166667}_{\text{duration charge}} \approx \$0.000133

content.text: A conversation requiring 10 reasoning steps costs roughly $0.0013 in Lambda compute — often far less than the LLM API token cost for the same conversation. Compared to running a reserved EC2 instance at $0.10/hour to serve that conversation, serverless can be 100x cheaper for low-to-medium traffic.
The architecture separates concerns across three layers.
(API Gateway, SQS, EventBridge, webhooks) deliver work items to the agent.
contain the agent logic: an orchestrator function that drives the reason-act loop and one or more tool-runner functions that execute individual tool calls in parallel.
— LLM APIs, database connectors, and the state store — are accessed over the network during each invocation and hold all durable data between invocations.

The orchestrator function is stateless in the FaaS sense: it reads state at the top of every invocation, performs exactly one reasoning step, writes updated state, and exits. The state store is the source of truth. Tool runners are even simpler — they receive a single tool input, call the external service, and return the result. This keeps each function small, fast to cold-start, and easy to test in isolation.

The response assembler (which may be the orchestrator itself in the final step, or a dedicated Lambda triggered by the final state transition) collects all tool results, formats the response, and delivers it to the caller — either by writing to a response queue that the client polls, pushing through a WebSocket connection maintained by API Gateway, or invoking a callback URL.
Bursty, unpredictable workloads: traffic patterns with wide variation between peak and trough, such as customer support, on-demand research, and interactive assistants.
Event-triggered agents: batch processing pipelines, nightly report generation, webhook-driven automation (responding to a GitHub issue, processing a new file upload).
Cost-sensitive deployments: startups and internal tooling where keeping infrastructure cost at zero during low-traffic periods matters.
Rapid iteration: teams shipping multiple agent versions per day benefit from instant deploys without service restarts.
Multi-tenant SaaS agents: running isolated function invocations per tenant avoids the noisy-neighbor problem inherent in shared long-running processes.
Ultra-low latency requirements: when every millisecond counts and cold starts of 200-800 ms are unacceptable, pre-warmed containers or always-on services are a better fit.
Local model inference: running a 70B parameter model on GPU requires persistent hardware that cannot be provisioned and released per-invocation at reasonable cost or speed.
Very long uninterruptible runs: agents that must run for hours without a safe checkpoint point cannot fit in a 15-minute Lambda window.
High-throughput token streaming: real-time token-by-token streaming to many concurrent users is easier to implement on a persistent WebSocket server than on stateless functions.
Define the data structure stored in DynamoDB or Redis for each session:
attribute so stale sessions expire automatically.

Keep it a thin coordinator: load state, call the LLM with the current messages and tool schema, parse the response, update state, and either dispatch tool runners or write the final answer. Implement a timeout guard that checks remaining execution time (
) and checkpoints if fewer than 5 seconds remain.
One function per tool category (web search, database, code execution). Each function accepts
, executes the tool, writes the result to the state store, and enqueues a re-invocation of the orchestrator.
Use SQS to trigger the orchestrator rather than synchronous HTTP calls. Set
to 3 on the main queue so transient failures retry, and route exhausted messages to a DLQ for human review.
Use DynamoDB on-demand capacity with a partition key of
. Enable TTL with a 7-day expiry. Use Redis (ElastiCache) as a read-through cache for active sessions to reduce DynamoDB read latency.
The orchestrator's execution role should have permission only to read/write its DynamoDB table, publish to its SQS queue, and invoke its tool-runner functions.
Use AWS SAM, CDK, or the Serverless Framework to define functions, queues, tables, and IAM policies together. Pin Lambda runtime versions and set reserved concurrency to cap unexpected spend.
Enable AWS X-Ray or OpenTelemetry. Propagate the
as a trace attribute so a full multi-step conversation can be reconstructed from a single trace query.
Example 1: Single-step orchestrator on AWS Lambda

import json
import os
import time
import boto3
import anthropic

dynamo = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table(os.environ["SESSIONS_TABLE"])
sqs    = boto3.client("sqs")
llm    = anthropic.Anthropic()

TOOLS = [
    {
        "name": "web_search",
        "description": "Search the web for current information.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
            "required": ["query"],
        },
    }
]

def handler(event, context):
    """Orchestrator Lambda: one agent reasoning step per invocation."""
    body       = json.loads(event["Records"][0]["body"])
    session_id = body["session_id"]
    user_msg   = body.get("user_message")

    resp  = dynamo.get_item(Key={"session_id": session_id})
    state = resp.get("Item", {
        "session_id": session_id,
        "messages":   [],
        "status":     "PENDING",
        "ttl":        int(time.time()) + 7 * 86400,
    })

    if user_msg:
        state["messages"].append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg})

    # Timeout guard: abort if less than 10 s remain
    if context.get_remaining_time_in_millis() < 10_000:
        state["status"] = "PENDING"
        dynamo.put_item(Item=state)
        return

    state["status"] = "REASONING"
    dynamo.put_item(Item=state)

    response = llm.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=4096,
        tools=TOOLS,
        messages=state["messages"],
    )

    if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
        tool_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
        state["messages"].append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
        state["status"] = "TOOL_DISPATCHED"
        state["pending_tools"] = [b.id for b in tool_blocks]
        dynamo.put_item(Item=state)
        for tool_block in tool_blocks:
            sqs.send_message(
                QueueUrl=os.environ["TOOL_QUEUE_URL"],
                MessageBody=json.dumps({
                    "session_id": session_id,
                    "tool_id":    tool_block.id,
                    "tool_name":  tool_block.name,
                    "tool_input": tool_block.input,
                }),
            )
    else:
        final_text = next(b.text for b in response.content if hasattr(b, "text"))
        state["messages"].append({"role": "assistant", "content": final_text})
        state["status"] = "DONE"
        dynamo.put_item(Item=state)
        sqs.send_message(
            QueueUrl=os.environ["RESPONSE_QUEUE_URL"],
            MessageBody=json.dumps({"session_id": session_id, "answer": final_text}),
        )
Example 2: Tool runner for web search

import json
import os
import boto3
import httpx

dynamo = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table(os.environ["SESSIONS_TABLE"])
sqs    = boto3.client("sqs")

def handler(event, context):
    """Tool Runner Lambda: executes one tool call, then re-triggers orchestrator."""
    body       = json.loads(event["Records"][0]["body"])
    session_id = body["session_id"]
    tool_id    = body["tool_id"]
    tool_name  = body["tool_name"]
    tool_input = body["tool_input"]

    result = _execute_tool(tool_name, tool_input)

    resp  = dynamo.get_item(Key={"session_id": session_id})
    state = resp["Item"]
    state["messages"].append({
        "role": "user",
        "content": [{
            "type":        "tool_result",
            "tool_use_id": tool_id,
            "content":     result,
        }]
    })
    state["pending_tools"] = [t for t in state.get("pending_tools", []) if t != tool_id]

    if not state["pending_tools"]:
        # All tools done — re-enqueue orchestrator for next reasoning step
        state["status"] = "PENDING"
        dynamo.put_item(Item=state)
        sqs.send_message(
            QueueUrl=os.environ["AGENT_QUEUE_URL"],
            MessageBody=json.dumps({"session_id": session_id}),
            MessageDeduplicationId=f"{session_id}-{len(state['messages'])}",
            MessageGroupId=session_id,
        )
    else:
        dynamo.put_item(Item=state)

def _execute_tool(name, input_data):
    if name == "web_search":
        resp = httpx.get(
            "https://api.search.example.com/search",
            params={"q": input_data["query"], "count": 5},
            headers={"X-API-Key": os.environ["SEARCH_API_KEY"]},
            timeout=10.0,
        )
        results = resp.json().get("results", [])
        return "\n\n".join(
            f"**{r['title']}**\n{r['snippet']}" for r in results[:3]
        )
    raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {name}")

A B2B SaaS company receives 500-2,000 customer support tickets per day, with 70% following patterns that can be resolved autonomously: password resets, invoice lookups, feature questions answered from documentation, and status queries. They deploy a serverless agent using Lambda and SQS that handles the full triage-and-response pipeline.

When a ticket arrives in Zendesk, a webhook fires an API Gateway endpoint that enqueues the ticket in SQS. The orchestrator Lambda reads the ticket, calls Claude with a system prompt that includes documentation snippets (retrieved via a vector-search Lambda invoked as a tool), checks the customer subscription status via a database tool, and drafts a response. If the agent's confidence score (returned in a structured output field) exceeds 0.85, the response is posted automatically; otherwise it is flagged for human review with a draft pre-filled.

At peak hours (10 concurrent Lambda invocations handling tickets simultaneously), the total Lambda cost for a month is under $15. The company previously paid for a t3.medium EC2 instance running a FastAPI server 24/7 at $30/month — half the compute cost — before accounting for the 70% reduction in human review time, which saved approximately 40 hours per month.
Storing session state in module-level variables
A very common mistake is caching session data in a module-level dict between invocations, assuming that the same execution environment will handle subsequent steps for the same session.

# WRONG: module-level state lost on cold start, shared across invocations
_sessions = {}

def handler(event, context):
    session_id = event["session_id"]
    state = _sessions.get(session_id, {"messages": []})  # silently loses state
    # process ...
    _sessions[session_id] = state  # may never be read again

# CORRECT: always load from external store at invocation start
import boto3
dynamo = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table(os.environ["SESSIONS_TABLE"])

def handler(event, context):
    session_id = event["session_id"]
    resp  = dynamo.get_item(Key={"session_id": session_id})
    state = resp.get("Item") or {"session_id": session_id, "messages": []}
    # process ...
    dynamo.put_item(Item=state)  # always write back
Running the entire multi-turn conversation in a single invocation
Looping through all reasoning steps inside one handler call hits the execution timeout for long conversations, and the entire run is lost if it crashes mid-loop with no checkpoint.
Not checking remaining execution time before starting an LLM call can leave the session in
status permanently if the function times out mid-call, with no recovery path.

# CORRECT: check remaining time before expensive LLM call
def handler(event, context):
    if context.get_remaining_time_in_millis() < 15_000:  # less than 15 seconds
        # Re-enqueue rather than risk a partial run
        requeue_session(event["session_id"])
        return
    response = llm.messages.create(...)
to prevent duplicate deliveries on network retries. Without it, the same agent step can be executed twice, causing duplicate tool calls or doubled responses. Use a stable key such as
Treat each Lambda call as a single iteration of the reason-act-observe loop. This maximizes checkpoint granularity and keeps timeout risk minimal.
Use SQS FIFO for session ordering.
FIFO queues with message group ID set to
ensure that steps within a session are processed in order, preventing race conditions between the orchestrator and tool runners writing to the same state record.
Generate a deterministic step key and store it in DynamoDB with a conditional write to detect and skip duplicate step executions.
Externalize secrets via AWS Secrets Manager.
Fetch API keys from Secrets Manager at cold start and cache in a module-level variable. Rotate secrets without redeploying the function.
Set reserved concurrency to cap spend.
Define a maximum concurrency limit on agent functions to prevent runaway costs during traffic spikes. Alert when the limit is hit rather than silently throttling without notification.
Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency or scheduled EventBridge pings to keep the orchestrator function warm during predictable high-traffic windows, eliminating cold starts during business hours.
Instrument every step with trace IDs.
as trace attributes in every log line and X-Ray segment. This makes reconstructing a full multi-step conversation trivial without log scraping.
Pin dependency versions and minimize package size.
Use Lambda Layers to share common packages across functions. Avoid bundling model weights or large inference libraries — use an external API instead. Smaller packages mean faster cold starts.
Cold start latency is the primary latency concern for user-facing serverless agents. Practical mitigations in order of impact:
Use the smallest runtime that works. Python 3.12 with minimal dependencies cold-starts faster than Python 3.10 with a full data-science stack.
Keep deployment packages under 5 MB unzipped. Lambda loads and extracts the package on cold start — large packages add hundreds of milliseconds.
Use Lambda SnapStart (Java) or Provisioned Concurrency (all runtimes) for latency-sensitive paths. SnapStart takes a snapshot of the initialized JVM and restores it, eliminating most cold start time.
Initialize heavy clients (boto3, Anthropic SDK, database connections) at module level so they are reused across warm invocations.
Concurrency and LLM rate limit coordination

AWS Lambda can scale to thousands of concurrent invocations. However, LLM providers impose rate limits (for example, 500,000 tokens per minute for tier-2 Claude accounts). Unbounded Lambda concurrency will trigger 429 rate limit errors from the LLM API. The solution is to use SQS batch sizes and visibility timeouts to naturally pace invocations, and to implement exponential backoff with jitter when a 429 is received:

Lambda allocates CPU proportionally to memory. An orchestrator function doing text processing and JSON serialization benefits from 512 MB to 1 GB. Tool-runner functions that make a single HTTP call can run at 256 MB. Timeout should be set to the 95th-percentile observed LLM response time plus a 20% buffer, typically 60-120 seconds for a single LLM call. Monitor the
metric in CloudWatch and adjust monthly as model response times change.
sam build and sam deploy define functions, queues, and tables in one template
Higher-level constructs for Lambda, SQS, and DynamoDB with IAM wired automatically
Plugins for GCP, Azure, and Lambda; serverless.yml declarative config
Built-in checkpointing to DynamoDB or Redis; maps naturally to per-step Lambda invocations
Streaming tool-use responses from Claude/GPT over Vercel Edge Functions
Sub-millisecond cold start; embedded Llama/Mistral for latency-sensitive agents
End-to-end trace across Lambda invocations; session_id as custom annotation
Structured logging, tracing, idempotency, and batch-processing helpers in Python and TypeScript
Metrics, traces, logs in one view
Serverless plugin injects tracing layer; anomaly detection on per-step duration

# Deploy a serverless agent stack with AWS SAM
sam init --runtime python3.12 --name agent-stack
# Edit template.yaml to define:
# - AgentOrchestratorFunction
# - ToolRunnerFunction
# - AgentQueue (SQS FIFO with DLQ)
# - SessionsTable (DynamoDB on-demand, TTL enabled)
# - IAM execution roles

sam build
sam deploy --guided   # creates CloudFormation stack on first run

# Subsequent deploys (seconds):
sam build && sam deploy
Fully managed infra, Google Cloud shops

Each function should have a dedicated IAM execution role with only the permissions it genuinely requires. The orchestrator needs: DynamoDB read/write on its session table; SQS send on the tool queue and response queue; Lambda invoke on tool-runner functions. It should have no permission to read S3 buckets belonging to other services, access other DynamoDB tables, or call arbitrary AWS services. Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to verify that no overly permissive actions are granted.

import boto3, json, os, functools

@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def get_secret(secret_id: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch and cache a secret at cold start; reused on warm invocations."""
    client  = boto3.client("secretsmanager", region_name=os.environ["AWS_REGION"])
    payload = client.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_id)
    return json.loads(payload["SecretString"])

# Called once per cold start, cached thereafter
secrets           = get_secret(os.environ["AGENT_SECRETS_ID"])
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = secrets["anthropic_api_key"]
SEARCH_API_KEY    = secrets["search_api_key"]
SQS delivers messages at least once, meaning a Lambda handler can be called multiple times for the same message if the function crashes mid-execution. Use Powertools for Lambda's
decorator (backed by DynamoDB) to detect and skip duplicate step executions:
All SQS queues feeding agent functions must have a DLQ. Set a CloudWatch alarm on the

metric of each DLQ with a threshold of 1 — any message landing in the DLQ should trigger an alert. Include enough context in the original SQS message body (session ID, step number, user ID) to reconstruct what failed without needing to dig through logs.
Input validation and prompt injection prevention

Serverless agents that accept user-supplied text are potential prompt injection targets. Validate and sanitize all inputs at the API Gateway layer using request validators. Use a structured output schema and reject responses that do not conform. Never construct the system prompt by directly concatenating user input — keep user content strictly in the user role messages.

Serverless agents map each reasoning step to one FaaS invocation and externalize all state to a durable store between invocations — this is the core architectural principle that makes stateful agents work on stateless infrastructure.
The pay-per-execution model is well-matched to bursty agent workloads; a conversation requiring 10 steps costs roughly $0.001 in Lambda compute, often less than the LLM API cost for the same session.
Cold starts (200 ms to 2 s) are the primary latency concern; mitigate with minimal packages, module-level client initialization, and Provisioned Concurrency for latency-sensitive paths.
Use SQS FIFO queues with per-session message group IDs to enforce step ordering and prevent race conditions between the orchestrator and tool runners.
Implement idempotency keys (using Powertools for Lambda or a conditional DynamoDB write) to handle SQS at-least-once delivery and prevent duplicate tool executions.
Reserve concurrency limits on agent functions and implement exponential backoff on LLM 429 responses to prevent runaway costs and cascading rate-limit failures.
Every SQS queue must have a dead-letter queue with a CloudWatch alarm on message visibility — a stuck session without a DLQ alert is invisible and unrecoverable.
Keep the orchestrator function a thin coordinator: one LLM call, one state write, one queue publish per invocation. Tool implementations belong in dedicated tool-runner functions.
Security discipline — IAM least privilege, Secrets Manager for API keys, structured output validation, and prompt injection defenses — is non-negotiable in production serverless agent deployments.
